question: Let $\vec{a}$ and $\vec{b}$ be two unit vectors such that the angle between them is $\frac{\pi}{3}$. If $\lambda \vec{a}+2 \vec{b}$ and $3 \vec{a}-\lambda \vec{b}$ are perpendicular to each other, then the number of values of $\lambda$ in $[-1,3]$ is :
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \hat{a} \cdot \hat{b}=\frac{1}{2} \\ & \text { Now }(\lambda \hat{a}+2 \hat{b}) \cdot(3 \hat{a}-\lambda \hat{b})=0 \\ & 3 \lambda \hat{a} \cdot \hat{a}-\lambda^2 \hat{a} \cdot \hat{b}+6 \hat{a} \cdot \hat{b}-2 \lambda \hat{b} \cdot \hat{b}=0 \\ & 3 \lambda-\frac{\lambda^2}{2}+3-2 \lambda=0 \\ & \lambda^2-2 \lambda-6=0 \\ & \lambda=1 \pm \sqrt{7} \\ & \Rightarrow \text { number of values }=0\end{aligned}$

q: If $\hat{a}, \hat{b}$ and $\hat{c}$ are unit vectors satisfying $\hat{a}-\sqrt{3} \hat{b}+\hat{c}=\overrightarrow{0}$, then the angle between the vectors $\hat{a}$ and $\hat{c}$ is :
question: If $\hat{a}, \hat{b}$ and $\hat{c}$ are unit vectors satisfying $\hat{a}-\sqrt{3} \hat{b}+\hat{c}=\overrightarrow{0}$, then the angle between the vectors $\hat{a}$ and $\hat{c}$ is :
solution:
Let angle between $\hat{a}$ and $\hat{c}$ be $\theta$.
Now, $\hat{a}-\sqrt{3} \hat{b}+\hat{c}=\overrightarrow{0}$
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \Rightarrow(\hat{a}+\hat{c})=\sqrt{3} \hat{b} \\
& \Rightarrow(\hat{a}+\hat{c}) \cdot(\hat{a}+\hat{c})=3(\hat{b} \cdot \hat{b}) \\
& \Rightarrow \hat{a} \cdot \hat{a}+\hat{a} \cdot \hat{c}+\hat{c} \cdot \hat{a}+\hat{c} \cdot \hat{c}=3 \times 1 \\
& \Rightarrow 1+2 \cos \theta+1=3 \\
& \Rightarrow \cos \theta=\frac{1}{2} \Rightarrow \theta=\frac{\pi}{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

q: Two adjacent sides of a parallelogram PQRS are given by $\vec{PQ} = \hat{j} + \hat{k}$ and $\vec{PS} = \hat{i} - \hat{j}$. If the side PS is rotated about the point P by an acute angle $\alpha$ in the plane of the parallelogram so that it becomes perpendicular to the side PQ, then $\sin^2\left(\dfrac{5\alpha}{2}\right) - \sin^2\left(\dfrac{\alpha}{2}\right)$ is equal to:
question: Two adjacent sides of a parallelogram PQRS are given by $\vec{PQ} = \hat{j} + \hat{k}$ and $\vec{PS} = \hat{i} - \hat{j}$. If the side PS is rotated about the point P by an acute angle $\alpha$ in the plane of the parallelogram so that it becomes perpendicular to the side PQ, then $\sin^2\left(\dfrac{5\alpha}{2}\right) - \sin^2\left(\dfrac{\alpha}{2}\right)$ is equal to:
solution:
Let $\theta$ be the angle between the vectors $\vec{PQ}$ and $\vec{PS}$.

$\cos \theta = \dfrac{\vec{PQ} \cdot \vec{PS}}{|\vec{PQ}| |\vec{PS}|}$

$\cos \theta = \dfrac{(\hat{j} + \hat{k}) \cdot (\hat{i} - \hat{j})}{\sqrt{1^2 + 1^2} \sqrt{1^2 + (-1)^2}}$

$\cos \theta = \dfrac{-1}{\sqrt{2} \times \sqrt{2}} = -\dfrac{1}{2}$

$\theta = 120^{\circ}$

The side PS is rotated by an acute angle $\alpha$ in the plane of the parallelogram to become perpendicular to PQ. The new angle between the sides is $90^{\circ}$.

$\alpha = 120^{\circ} - 90^{\circ} = 30^{\circ}$

The given expression is $\sin^2\left(\dfrac{5\alpha}{2}\right) - \sin^2\left(\dfrac{\alpha}{2}\right)$.

Using the trigonometric identity $\sin^2 A - \sin^2 B = \sin(A+B) \sin(A-B)$:

$\sin^2\left(\dfrac{5\alpha}{2}\right) - \sin^2\left(\dfrac{\alpha}{2}\right) = \sin\left(\dfrac{5\alpha}{2} + \dfrac{\alpha}{2}\right) \sin\left(\dfrac{5\alpha}{2} - \dfrac{\alpha}{2}\right)$

$= \sin(3\alpha) \sin(2\alpha)$

Substituting $\alpha = 30^{\circ}$:

$= \sin(90^{\circ}) \sin(60^{\circ})$

$= 1 \times \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$

Answer: $\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$

solution: $\begin{aligned} & \because \vec{a}+2 \vec{b}+2 \overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}=\overrightarrow{0} \text { [Given] } \\ \Rightarrow & \vec{a}+2 \overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}=-2 \vec{b} \\ \Rightarrow &(\vec{a}+2 \mathrm{c} \overrightarrow{)} \cdot(\vec{a}+2 \overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}})=(-2 \vec{b})(-2 \overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}})\\ \Rightarrow \vec{a} \cdot \vec{a}+4 \vec{c} \cdot \vec{c}+4 \vec{a} \cdot \vec{c}=4 \vec{b} \cdot \vec{b} \\ \Rightarrow & 1+4+4 \vec{a} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}=4 \\ \Rightarrow & \vec{a} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}=\frac{-1}{4} \\ \because &|\vec{a} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}|^2+|\vec{a} \times \vec{c}|^2=1(\vec{a} \text { is unit vector }) \\ & \frac{1}{16}+|\vec{a} \times \vec{c}|^2=1 \\ &|\vec{a} \times \vec{c}|^2=\frac{15}{16} \\ &|\vec{a} \times \vec{c}|^2=\frac{\sqrt{15}}{4} \end{aligned}$

question: If $\left|\begin{array}{lll}a & a^2 & 1+a^3 \\ b & b^2 & 1+b^3 \\ c & c^2 & 1+c^3\end{array}\right|=0$ and vectors $\left(1, a, a^2\right),\left(a, b, b^2\right)$ and $\left(a, c, c^2\right)$ are non-coplanar, then the product abc equals
solution:
$\left|\begin{array}{lll}\mathrm{a} & \mathrm{a}^2 & 1+\mathrm{a}^3 \\ \mathrm{~b} & \mathrm{~b}^2 & 1+\mathrm{b}^3 \\ \mathrm{c} & \mathrm{c}^2 & 1+\mathrm{c}^3\end{array}\right|=0 \Rightarrow\left|\begin{array}{lll}\mathrm{a} & \mathrm{a}^2 & 1 \\ \mathrm{~b} & \mathrm{~b}^2 & 1 \\ \mathrm{c} & \mathrm{c}^2 & 1\end{array}\right|+\left|\begin{array}{ccc}\mathrm{a} & \mathrm{a}^2 & \mathrm{a}^3 \\ \mathrm{~b} & \mathrm{~b}^2 & \mathrm{~b}^3 \\ \mathrm{c} & \mathrm{c}^2 & \mathrm{c}^3\end{array}\right|=0$
$(a-b)(b-c)(c-a)+a b c(a-b)(b-c)(c-a)=0$
$(a b c+1)[(a-b)(b-c)(c-a)]=0$
As $\left|\begin{array}{lll}1 & \mathrm{a} & \mathrm{a}^2 \\ 1 & \mathrm{~b} & \mathrm{~b}^2 \\ 1 & \mathrm{c} & \mathrm{c}^2\end{array}\right| \neq 0$ (given condition) $\quad \therefore \mathrm{abc}=-1$

q: Let $\vec{a_k} = (\tan\theta_k)\hat{i} + \hat{j}$ and $\vec{b_k} = \hat{i} - (\cot\theta_k)\hat{j}$, where $\theta_k = \dfrac{2^{k-1}\pi}{2^n + 1}$, for some $n \in \mathbb{N}$, $n > 5$. Then the value of $\dfrac{\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^{n}|\vec{a_k}|^2}{\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^{n}|\vec{b_k}|^2}$ is _____.
question: Let $\vec{a_k} = (\tan\theta_k)\hat{i} + \hat{j}$ and $\vec{b_k} = \hat{i} - (\cot\theta_k)\hat{j}$, where $\theta_k = \dfrac{2^{k-1}\pi}{2^n + 1}$, for some $n \in \mathbb{N}$, $n > 5$. Then the value of $\dfrac{\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^{n}|\vec{a_k}|^2}{\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^{n}|\vec{b_k}|^2}$ is _____.
solution:
Let $\vec{a}_k = \tan\theta_k\,\hat{i} + \hat{j}$ and $\vec{b}_k = \hat{i} - \cot\theta_k\,\hat{j}$, where $\theta_k = \dfrac{2^{k-1}\pi}{2^n + 1}$.

$|\vec{a}_k|^2 = \tan^2\theta_k + 1 = \sec^2\theta_k$
$|\vec{b}_k|^2 = 1 + \cot^2\theta_k = \csc^2\theta_k$

So the required ratio is:
$\dfrac{\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^n |\vec{a}_k|^2}{\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^n |\vec{b}_k|^2} = \dfrac{\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^n \sec^2\theta_k}{\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^n \csc^2\theta_k}$

Relation between successive angles:
$\theta_{k+1} = \dfrac{2^k\pi}{2^n + 1} = 2\theta_k$

Key identity:
$\sec^2\theta + \csc^2\theta = \dfrac{1}{\cos^2\theta} + \dfrac{1}{\sin^2\theta} = \dfrac{1}{\sin^2\theta\cos^2\theta} = \dfrac{4}{\sin^2 2\theta} = 4\csc^2 2\theta$

Hence:
$\sec^2\theta = 4\csc^2 2\theta - \csc^2\theta$

Evaluating the numerator using this identity and $2\theta_k = \theta_{k+1}$:
$\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^n \sec^2\theta_k = \sum_{k=1}^n \left(4\csc^2\theta_{k+1} - \csc^2\theta_k\right) = 4\sum_{k=1}^n \csc^2\theta_{k+1} - \sum_{k=1}^n \csc^2\theta_k$

Let $S_b = \displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^n \csc^2\theta_k$. Then:
$\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^n \csc^2\theta_{k+1} = S_b - \csc^2\theta_1 + \csc^2\theta_{n+1}$

So:
$\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^n \sec^2\theta_k = 4\left(S_b - \csc^2\theta_1 + \csc^2\theta_{n+1}\right) - S_b = 3S_b - 4\csc^2\theta_1 + 4\csc^2\theta_{n+1}$

Boundary terms:
$\theta_1 = \dfrac{\pi}{2^n + 1}$, $\theta_{n+1} = \dfrac{2^n\pi}{2^n + 1}$

$\theta_1 + \theta_{n+1} = \dfrac{\pi + 2^n\pi}{2^n + 1} = \dfrac{(2^n + 1)\pi}{2^n + 1} = \pi$

So $\theta_{n+1} = \pi - \theta_1$, and since $\csc(\pi - x) = \csc x$:
$\csc^2\theta_{n+1} = \csc^2\theta_1$

Therefore, $-4\csc^2\theta_1 + 4\csc^2\theta_{n+1} = 0$, and:
$\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^n \sec^2\theta_k = 3S_b = 3\sum_{k=1}^n \csc^2\theta_k$

Hence, the required ratio is:
$\dfrac{3S_b}{S_b} = 3$

Therefore, the answer is $3$.

q: If $|a|=5,|b|=4,|c|=3$ thus what will be the value of $|a \cdot b+b . c+c . a|$, given that $\vec{a}+\vec{b}+\vec{c}=0$
question: If $|a|=5,|b|=4,|c|=3$ thus what will be the value of $|a \cdot b+b . c+c . a|$, given that $\vec{a}+\vec{b}+\vec{c}=0$
solution:
We have, $\vec{a}+\vec{b}+\vec{c}=\overrightarrow{0} \Rightarrow(\vec{a}+\vec{b}+\vec{c})^2=0$
$\Rightarrow|\vec{a}|^2+|\vec{b}|^2+|\vec{c}|^2+2(\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}+\vec{b} \cdot \vec{c}+\vec{c} \cdot \vec{a})=0 \Rightarrow 25+16+9+2(\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}+\vec{b} \cdot \vec{c}+\vec{c} \cdot \vec{a})=0$
$\Rightarrow(\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}+\vec{b} \cdot \vec{c}+\vec{c} \cdot \vec{a})=-25 \quad \therefore|\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}+\vec{b} \cdot \vec{c}+\vec{c} \cdot \vec{a}|=25$
